concept.headline: Kotlin classes declare properties directly in the primary constructor. Methods are functions inside the class body. Classes are final by default.
concept.explanation: A Kotlin class's primary constructor sits right after the class name. Properties declared with val/var there are automatically created with getters (and setters for var). The class body holds methods and additional properties. Unlike Java there is no need to write field declarations and a constructor separately. Classes cannot be subclassed unless marked open.
keyPoints: class Name(val prop: Type, var mutableProp: Type)
val in constructor → getter only. var → getter + setter.
Member functions access properties with this (often implicit)
Classes are final by default — use open to allow inheritance

demo.intro: Watch a Person class being constructed and its methods called.

explanation.why: Primary constructor parameters become the object's properties automatically
explanation.misconception: There is no separate init block needed here — the primary constructor parameters marked val/var ARE the property declarations. No extra code required.

explanation.what: p.greet() — calling the member function on p
explanation.why: Access the object's method using dot notation
explanation.intent: Show member function accessing the object's own properties
explanation.misconception: Inside greet, $name and $age refer to this.name and this.age — the implicit receiver. this is optional and usually omitted.

explanation.why: greet() assembled the string template using p's current properties
explanation.intent: Show how member functions use property values
explanation.misconception: greet() is a pure function — it reads but does not modify any property

explanation.what: p.birthday() — mutates the object's age property
explanation.why: birthday increments age by 1 — it modifies the mutable var property
explanation.intent: Show that var properties can be mutated through methods
explanation.misconception: birthday() can mutate age because age is declared with var. If age were val, the age++ inside birthday() would be a compile error.

explanation.intent: Confirm mutation via method was reflected in the property
explanation.misconception: p.age accesses the property directly — no getter call needed in Kotlin syntax, though internally it calls the generated getter

prediction.question:
Given:

class Box(val width: Int, val height: Int)
val b = Box(4, 6)
b.width = 10

What happens?
options: Compile error: Val cannot be reassigned
prediction.explanation: width is declared with val, so its setter is never generated. Trying to assign b.width = 10 is a compile error — 'Val cannot be reassigned'. Change val to var in the constructor to allow mutation.

explanation.afterDemo: Kotlin's primary constructor eliminates the Java ceremony of declaring fields, writing constructors, and writing getters. You express the same thing in one concise line. val properties are read-only from outside the class; var ones are mutable. This makes class APIs immediately readable — you can tell at a glance what can be changed.
keyTakeaways: Primary constructor: class Name(val x: T, var y: T) — properties declared inline.
val generates a getter only; var generates getter + setter.
Member functions access properties via this (implicit) — no boilerplate needed.

practice.prompt: Create a class Rectangle(val width: Int, val height: Int) with a function area(): Int that returns width * height. In main, create Rectangle(4, 5) and print area().
practice.hint: Declare width and height as val in the primary constructor. area() = width * height as expression body.

retryHints: class Rectangle(val width: Int, val height: Int)

summary.title: Classes — Complete
takeaways: Primary constructor declares properties inline — no separate field declarations.
val property → read-only (getter only). var property → mutable (getter + setter).
Member functions access properties directly — this is implicit.
